--- Page 3 ---
Chapter 1
Problem Statement and Motivation
In modern organizations, critical knowledge is stored in documents such as PDFs, re-
ports, manuals, and internal documentation. Extracting precise information from these
documents is often time-consuming and inefficient.
Existing solutions face key limitations:
• Manual document search is slow and error-prone
• Keyword-based search lacks semantic understanding
• Generic chatbots hallucinate responses
• Answers lack verifiable document grounding
The motivation behind MindDoc AI was to solve this problem by building a stable,
document-aware conversational system that provides accurate, explainable, and trust-
worthy answers derived strictly from user-uploaded documents.
2

4.text:
--- Page 4 ---
Chapter 2
What MindDoc AI Does
MindDoc AI is a document-centric conversational platform that allows users to upload
documents and interact with them using natural language queries.
Core features include:
• PDF document upload and persistent storage
• Text extraction and preprocessing
• Conversational querying of document content
• Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
• Stored chat sessions and document context
Unlike generic chat interfaces, MindDoc AI ensures that responses are grounded
strictly in uploaded documents, making it suitable for enterprise and professional en-
vironments.
3

5.text:
--- Page 5 ---
Chapter 3
How the Project Was Built
The project was developed using a stability-first engineering philosophy inspired by
real-world enterprise systems.
Guiding principles:
• Stability over intelligence
• Fail fast, fail clearly
• Single execution path
• Easy debugging and predictability
The system deliberately avoids retries, fallback models, background recovery, and
self-healing logic to maintain clarity and reliability.
4

--- Page 6 ---
Chapter 4
Technical Architecture Overview
MindDoc AI follows a modular client-server architecture.
High-Level Components
• Frontend: User interface and interaction layer
• Backend: Business logic and API handling
• Storage: Persistent documents and metadata
• LLM Layer: Controlled document-grounded response generation
This architecture balances simplicity with professional structure.
5

6.text:
--- Page 7 ---
Chapter 5
Retrieval-Augmented Generation
(RAG)
Retrieval-Augmented Generation combines document retrieval with language model gen-
eration.
RAG Flow
User Query
↓
Relevant Document Retrieval
↓
Context Injection
↓
LLM Response
RAG ensures that answers are:
• Accurate
• Explainable
• Document-grounded

--- Page 8 ---
Chapter 6
Tools and Technologies Used
6.1
Frontend Technologies
6.1.1
React with TypeScript
React provides a component-based architecture suitable for dynamic UIs. TypeScript
adds static typing, improving reliability and maintainability.
Responsibilities:
• Dashboard rendering
• Document management UI
• Chat interface
6.2
Backend Technologies
6.2.1
FastAPI
FastAPI was chosen for:
• High performance
• Strong typing
• Automatic API documentation
The backend manages authentication, document processing, chat sessions, and RAG
execution.
7

7.text:
--- Page 9 ---
6.3
Database and Storage
6.3.1
SQLite
SQLite was selected due to:
• Lightweight nature
• No external server dependency
• Reliability for resume-grade systems
6.3.2
File System Storage
Uploaded documents are stored on disk, ensuring persistence across restarts.
6.4
Document Processing
6.4.1
PDF Text Extraction
PDFs are parsed deterministically to extract text for indexing. No repeated or unneces-
sary processing occurs.
6.4.2
Text Chunking
Extracted text is divided into logical chunks to improve retrieval accuracy and LLM
context handling.
6.5
Vector Retrieval
Document chunks are indexed using vector similarity search, enabling fast semantic re-
trieval.
6.6
LLM Integration
MindDoc AI uses:
• Ollama as the LLM runtime
• DeepSeek as the single configured model
Design constraints:
• One model only

Chat Session Management
Chat sessions are stored and persisted, allowing users to:

• Delete sessions individually
This treats conversations as work artifacts rather than ephemeral messages.

Dashboard and User Experience
The dashboard presents:
• Recent documents
• Saved (starred) documents
• Chat sessions
Design philosophy:
• Professional
• Calm
• Enterprise-grade
No emojis or playful elements are used.

System Stability Philosophy

• Background workers
• Automatic retries
• Model switching
This ensures predictable, debuggable behavior aligned with real enterprise systems.

spiritual, and practical learning in human history. Rooted in values, discipline, and holistic
development, it aimed not only at knowledge acquisition but also the shaping of moral character and

were not just instructors but mentors who guided students in ethical, intellectual, and emotional

came from all over Asia-China, Japan, Korea, Tibet, and even Central Asia. Famous travelers such

research university with specialized faculties, residential facilities, and administrative departments. It
represented the ideal of "universal education," blending theory and practice, philosophy and

Takshashila was destroyed by the Huns around the 5th century CE, and Nalanda was burned down

manuscripts were lost forever. The destruction of these centers marked a tragic setback to global
education and scientific advancement.
Despite their fall, the legacy of Nalanda and Takshashila continues to inspire modern education.
Their emphasis on holistic learning, intellectual freedom, and international collaboration mirrors the
goals of contemporary universities. Modern institutions like Nalanda University (revived in 2014) aim
to rekindle this spirit by promoting interdisciplinary research and cultural exchange.

Assignment-I: Internet Security

1. Role of Hardware-Based Security, Software Firewalls, and Security Standards

Hardware-Based Security: Hardware-based security uses physical devices such as security modules, Trusted Platform Modules (TPM), and network appliances (like hardware firewalls) to safeguard data and systems. These provide encryption, secure boot, and tamper detection, reducing the risk of OS-level breaches. Hardware security ensures isolation from software-based attacks and protects keys or credentials at the hardware level.

Software Firewalls: Software firewalls are installed on endpoints or servers to monitor and filter network traffic based on defined rules. They can detect malicious activities such as unauthorized access, port scanning, or malware communication. Examples include Windows Defender Firewall and ZoneAlarm. Software firewalls are flexible and can be updated easily but may consume system resources.

40.text:
Security Standards: Security standards such as ISO/IEC 27001, NIST, and CIS provide frameworks for implementing and maintaining secure systems. These standards help organizations establish access control, encryption, incident response, and compliance measures to mitigate both application-level and OS-level threats.

Evaluation: A hybrid approach combining hardware-based security (for protection at the physical and encryption level) and software firewalls (for network-level and application-layer defense) offers the best protection. Hardware ensures low-level integrity, while software adds adaptability and centralized management.


2. Cyber Incident Response Plan (CIRP) for a Mid-Size IT Company

Steps for Assessing Threat Levels:
Identify the nature of the incident (malware, phishing, data breach).
Assess scope, severity, and potential impact on confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
Classify threat level as Low, Medium, or High.

Procedure for Reporting Cybercrime:

Resilience Measures:
Conduct regular penetration tests.
Patch systems and enforce least-privilege access.

Provide employee security awareness training.




3. Comparison of Network Reconnaissance and Sniffing Tools

Reconnaissance Tools (Nmap, THC-Amap):

42.text:
3. Comparison of Network Reconnaissance and Sniffing Tools

Reconnaissance Tools (Nmap, THC-Amap):

Nmap: Scans networks to identify hosts, open ports, and services. It helps detect vulnerable systems and misconfigured services.
THC-Amap: Focuses on application-level service detection by matching response patterns even on non-standard ports.

Sniffing Tools (Wireshark, Tcpdump, Ettercap, Kismet):

Wireshark: Captures and analyzes network packets for deep inspection.
Tcpdump: Command-line tool for capturing and filtering packets.
Ettercap: Used for man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks and real-time packet analysis.
Kismet: Specialized in wireless network detection and packet sniffing.

43.text:
Comparison: Reconnaissance focuses on identifying systems and open services, while sniffing inspects data flow between systems. Both can identify vulnerabilities: reconnaissance exposes attack surfaces, while sniffing reveals insecure communication.
Application: Used together, these tools help administrators secure networks by closing unnecessary ports, encrypting traffic, and detecting intrusion attempts.



4. Step-by-Step Vulnerability Scanning Process
Tools Used: Netcat, Socat, Fpipe, Wireshark
Steps:

44.text:
4. Step-by-Step Vulnerability Scanning Process
Tools Used: Netcat, Socat, Fpipe, Wireshark
Steps:

Network Mapping: Identify active hosts and IP ranges.
Open Port Identification: Use Netcat to scan and detect open ports.
Banner Grabbing: Extract service banners to identify software versions.
Service Verification: Use Socat or Fpipe for port redirection and testing response behavior.
Vulnerability Probing: Compare identified versions with known vulnerabilities (via CVE databases).
Packet Capture and Analysis: Use Wireshark to monitor scanning traffic and verify responses.
Reporting: Document detected weaknesses, prioritize by severity, and suggest mitigations.

Integration Workflow:
Open Port Scanning → Banner Checks → Vulnerability Probes → Verification → Reporting

Diagram/Flowchart (Text Representation):
Network Hosts → Port Scanning (Netcat) → Banner Check (Socat/Fpipe) → Vulnerability Probe → Packet Analysis (Wireshark) → Report Findings

Assignment-II: Internet Security

1. Firewall and Packet-Filtering Plan

Scenario: Organization with public web and email servers, and a private internal database.

Firewall Design: A stateful firewall is preferred since it tracks the state of network connections, allowing dynamic packet filtering and better intrusion detection compared to stateless firewalls.

Packet Filtering Parameters:
Source and destination IP address
Port numbers (e.g., block unused ports, allow 80/443 for web, 25/587 for mail)
Protocol type (TCP/UDP/ICMP)
Direction of traffic (inbound/outbound)
Packet flags and size anomalies
NAT and Port Forwarding:

NAT (Network Address Translation): Masks internal IPs to prevent direct external access.
Port Forwarding: Allows controlled external access to public web (HTTP/HTTPS) and email (SMTP/IMAP) servers.

Network Diagram (Text Representation):
Internet → Firewall (Stateful) → DMZ (Web & Email Servers) → Internal Network (Database Server, Private Systems)

1. Digital Evidence Collection and Preservation
Evidence Collection Process:

Identification: Determine the type of evidence (logs, hard drives, emails).
Acquisition: Create a bit-by-bit copy of storage media using forensic tools (FTK Imager, EnCase).
Preservation: Maintain integrity by using write blockers and calculating hash values (MD5/SHA-1).
Documentation: Record details like date, time, and investigator.
Evidence Preservation Steps:
Use secure, tamper-proof storage.
Maintain chain of custody with digital signatures.
Restrict access to authorized personnel only.
Recovering Deleted Evidence: Tools like Autopsy, Recuva, and EnCase can recover deleted files or fragments from unallocated space.

Email Tracking: Analyze email headers to trace the sender's IP and server path.

IP Tracking: Use WHOIS or traceroute to locate the origin of the email.

Email Recovery: Retrieve deleted emails from server backups or local archives.

Encryption/Decryption: Use forensic tools to decode encrypted attachments or messages.

Search and Seizure Procedures: Follow legal guidelines under IT Act 2000 during evidence collection.

Password Cracking: Use tools like John the Ripper ethically with permission to access protected files.

Chapter 1
Problem Statement and Motivation
In modern organizations, critical knowledge is stored in documents such as PDFs, re-
ports, manuals, and internal documentation. Extracting precise information from these
documents is often time-consuming and inefficient.
Existing solutions face key limitations:
• Manual document search is slow and error-prone
• Keyword-based search lacks semantic understanding
• Generic chatbots hallucinate responses
• Answers lack verifiable document grounding
The motivation behind MindDoc AI was to solve this problem by building a stable,
document-aware conversational system that provides accurate, explainable, and trust-
worthy answers derived strictly from user-uploaded documents.
2

Chapter 2
What MindDoc AI Does
MindDoc AI is a document-centric conversational platform that allows users to upload
documents and interact with them using natural language queries.
Core features include:
• PDF document upload and persistent storage
• Text extraction and preprocessing
• Conversational querying of document content
• Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
• Stored chat sessions and document context
Unlike generic chat interfaces, MindDoc AI ensures that responses are grounded
strictly in uploaded documents, making it suitable for enterprise and professional en-
vironments.

The system deliberately avoids retries, fallback models, background recovery, and

Background Theory
3.1 Socket Programming
A socket is a software abstraction that provides an endpoint for communication between two processes over a network. The 
socket API was originally developed for Unix and has since become the standard interface for network programming across 
all major operating systems. In Python, the socket module wraps the underlying OS socket calls and makes them accessible 
through a simple object-oriented interface.
A socket is identified by two properties: an IP address, which identifies the machine, and a port number, which identifies the 
specific process or application on that machine. Our server uses port 12345 to listen for incoming connections.
3.2 TCP Protocol
TCP stands for Transmission Control Protocol. It is a connection-oriented protocol that operates at Layer 4 (Transport Layer) 
of the OSI model. TCP provides reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of data between applications. It achieves this

105.text:
of the OSI model. TCP provides reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of data between applications. It achieves this 
through the following mechanisms:
Three-way handshake 4 SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK 4 to establish a connection
Sequence numbers to ensure data arrives in the correct order
Acknowledgement (ACK) messages to confirm delivery
Retransmission of lost packets
For a chat application, TCP is the correct choice because every message must arrive completely and in order. An alternative 
protocol, UDP (User Datagram Protocol), is faster but does not guarantee delivery or ordering, making it unsuitable for chat.
3.3 Client-Server Architecture
In the Client-Server model, one process (the server) waits for connection requests from other processes (clients). The server 
is always running and ready to accept new connections, while clients initiate the communication. In our chat application, the

106.text:
is always running and ready to accept new connections, while clients initiate the communication. In our chat application, the 
server acts as a central hub: every message sent by a client is received by the server and forwarded to all other connected 
clients. This is known as broadcasting.
3.4 Multi-Threading
A thread is the smallest unit of a process that can be scheduled and executed independently. Python's threading module 
allows a program to run multiple threads concurrently. In our server, each time a new client connects, a dedicated thread is 
created for that client using threading.Thread(). This allows the server to handle many clients at the same time without 
blocking 4 when Thread 1 is waiting for Alice to send a message, Thread 2 can still handle Bob's messages independently.

import socket
import threading
HOST = '127.0.0.1' # must match the server

nickname = input('Enter your nickname: ')
# create socket and connect to server
client = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
client.connect((HOST, PORT))
# receive() runs in a background thread
# it listens for incoming messages from the server
def receive():
 while True:
 try:

 except:

 break
# send() runs on the main thread
# it reads keyboard input and sends it to the server
def send():
 while True:

 message = f'{nickname}: {input("")}'
 client.send(message.encode('utf-8'))
 except:
 client.close()
 break
# start both threads at the same time

The project successfully demonstrates the practical implementation of a Client-Server chat system using Python socket 
programming. A few observations worth noting from the testing are described below.
The use of Python's threading module was essential for making the server handle multiple clients without blocking. During

122.text:
The use of Python's threading module was essential for making the server handle multiple clients without blocking. During 
testing, it was observed that when a third client (Charlie) was connected while Alice and Bob were already chatting, all three 
users could exchange messages without any delay or interference. This confirms that each thread operates independently.
One limitation noticed during testing is that if the server is stopped while clients are still connected, the clients receive a 
connection error on their next recv() call. The current implementation handles this gracefully by catching the exception and 
printing a disconnection message, but in a production-level application it would be better to notify the user more clearly and 
offer a reconnect option.

panel is particularly useful for demonstrating how the application-level data (our chat message) gets wrapped in TCP and IP 
headers as it travels down the OSI stack.

4.3 Message Flow
The sequence below describes how a message sent by Alice reaches Bob:
Alice types a message in her terminal and presses Enter
The send() function reads the input, prefixes her nickname, and calls .encode('utf-8') to convert the string to bytes
socket.send() transmits the bytes to the server over the established TCP connection
The server's handle_client() thread for Alice calls socket.recv(1024) which returns the incoming bytes
The server calls broadcast(), which loops through the clients[] list and calls socket.send() on each socket
Bob's receive() thread calls socket.recv(1024), decodes the bytes using .decode('utf-8'), and prints the message

Cisco Packet Tracer Setup

6.1 Steps to Build the Topology
The following steps were carried out to create and test the network simulation in Cisco Packet Tracer 8.x:

Step 1 — Add Devices
Four PC end devices and one Cisco Catalyst 2960 Switch were dragged from the device panel onto the workspace. The PCs were renamed PC-Server, PC-Alice, PC-Bob and PC-Charlie.

Step 2 — Connect Devices
Copper Straight-Through cables were used to connect each PC to the switch. PC-Alice was connected to Fa0/1, PC-Bob to Fa0/2, PC-Charlie to Fa0/3 and PC-Server to Fa0/4. After a few seconds, the link indicators turned green confirming the connections were active.

Step 3 — Assign IP Addresses
Each PC was configured with a static IP address through Desktop → IP Configuration. The subnet mask 255.255.255.0 was used for all devices, placing them on the same network (192.168.1.0/24).

140.text:
Step 4 — Connectivity Test (Ping)
To verify that the network was correctly configured, a ping test was run from PC-Alice to PC-Server using the Command Prompt in the Desktop tab. The result showed four successful replies with 0% packet loss, confirming that Alice could reach the server across the switch.


Step 5 — PDU Simulation
The simulation was switched to Simulation Mode using the clock icon. A Simple PDU was added from PC-Alice (source) to PC-Server (destination). The Play button was used to animate the packet travelling from Alice through Switch0 to the Server. Clicking on the packet envelope displayed the full PDU detail window showing all OSI layers including the TCP segment at Layer 4 with destination port 12345, and the IP packet at Layer 3 with destination address 192.168.1.1.

8. Discussion
The project successfully demonstrates the practical implementation of a Client-Server chat system using Python socket programming. A few observations worth noting from the testing are described below.

142.text:
The use of Python's threading module was essential for making the server handle multiple clients without blocking. During testing, it was observed that when a third client (Charlie) was connected while Alice and Bob were already chatting, all three users could exchange messages without any delay or interference. This confirms that each thread operates independently.

One limitation noticed during testing is that if the server is stopped while clients are still connected, the clients receive a connection error on their next recv() call. The current implementation handles this gracefully by catching the exception and printing a disconnection message, but in a production-level application it would be better to notify the user more clearly and offer a reconnect option.

143.text:
The Cisco Packet Tracer simulation, while limited to a single PDU simulation rather than real TCP data, effectively visualises the layer-by-layer encapsulation of data as it travels from a client PC to the server PC through the switch. The PDU detail panel is particularly useful for demonstrating how the application-level data (our chat message) gets wrapped in TCP and IP headers as it travels down the OSI stack.

9. Conclusion
This project has successfully implemented a Chat Application that demonstrates several important Computer Networks concepts in a practical, working system. The Python implementation covers socket programming, the Client-Server model, TCP/IP communication, and multi-threaded programming — all using only the standard library with no external dependencies.

144.text:
The Cisco Packet Tracer simulation provides a complementary visual representation of the network infrastructure, showing how IP addressing, switching, and packet transmission work at the hardware and protocol level. Together, the two components cover the complete picture from the physical cable (Layer 1) through to the running application (Layer 7).

The project can be further extended by adding features such as a graphical user interface using tkinter, user authentication with a username and password, message encryption using AES or RSA, support for file transfer between users, and the ability to create private chat rooms within the same server.
